---
title: "LLM Hardware Calculators"
---

Several online calculators can help estimate whether a large language model will run on a given machine, how much memory it will require, and in some cases how much throughput to expect.

This chapter focuses on the most useful calculators for practical **inference planning**, especially when comparing models, quantization levels, context lengths, and deployment patterns.

## Top picks

### 1. ApX ML VRAM Calculator

<https://apxml.com/tools/vram-calculator>

This is the most broadly useful calculator in the group. It supports both conventional GPU setups and Apple Silicon, and it estimates not only memory usage but also expected tokens-per-second. It also accounts for quantization, memory bandwidth, and GPU-specific performance differences.

For general planning, this is the strongest first stop.

### 2. Hugging Face LLM-Model-VRAM-Calculator (NyxKrage)

<https://huggingface.co/spaces/NyxKrage/LLM-Model-VRAM-Calculator>

This one is especially useful when evaluating a **specific Hugging Face model**. You can provide the model URL, choose quantization, adjust context length, and optionally enter hardware details.

It is a good cross-check when your planning starts from a concrete model on the Hugging Face Hub rather than from a general hardware budget.

### 3. SelfHostLLM

<https://selfhostllm.org/>

SelfHostLLM is more deployment-oriented. It focuses on self-hosted inference and, importantly, estimates **maximum concurrent requests** in addition to raw memory usage.

Its planning logic is centered around the idea that:

```text
Available Memory = Total VRAM - System Overhead - Model Memory
Max Requests = Available Memory / KV Cache per Request
```

That makes it especially useful when the question is not just "Can this model run?" but also "How many simultaneous users or requests can this deployment support?"

## Also worth knowing

### 4. LocalLLM.in VRAM Calculator

<https://localllm.in/blog/interactive-vram-calculator>

This calculator aims for more precise memory estimation and claims close agreement with real `llama.cpp` measurements. It also accounts for features such as grouped-query attention and newer architecture details.

It is useful when precision matters more than convenience.

### 5. Onyx, What LLM Can I Run?

<https://onyx.app/llm-hardware-requirements>

Onyx is optimized for speed and convenience. It auto-detects hardware, estimates model speed and memory usage, and helps shortlist models that are likely to run well on the current system.

This is one of the fastest ways to move from hardware inspection to a plausible model shortlist.

## Feature comparison

| Calculator | Apple Silicon | TPS estimate | Hugging Face model input | Concurrency |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| ApX ML | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | - |
| HF NyxKrage | - | - | ✓ | - |
| SelfHostLLM | - | - | - | ✓ |
| LocalLLM.in | - | - | ✓ | - |
| Onyx | ✓ (auto) | ✓ | - | - |

## A practical recommendation

A pragmatic workflow looks like this:

1. Start with **ApX ML** for broad planning, especially when comparing hardware classes, quantization options, and rough throughput.
2. Cross-check with **HF NyxKrage** when evaluating a specific model from the Hugging Face Hub.
3. Use **SelfHostLLM** when concurrency and serving capacity matter.
4. Use **LocalLLM.in** when you want a more measurement-oriented estimate.
5. Use **Onyx** when you want a fast shortlist of likely runnable models.

In practice, **ApX ML + HF NyxKrage** will cover most single-user model selection questions, while **SelfHostLLM** becomes more important when moving toward shared or production-style deployments.

## Why these calculators matter

Hardware calculators are useful because LLM feasibility depends on multiple interacting variables:

- parameter count
- quantization level
- context length
- KV-cache growth
- memory bandwidth
- concurrency target

A single static rule such as "7B fits in X GB" is often too crude. These calculators help turn those variables into more realistic planning decisions before testing a real deployment.
